reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Egyensúly Intézet's documented commitment to 'okos nemzet' (smart nation) founded on talent-development equity: the 2021 unified primary model presumes early identification must not be SES-contaminated. S1's admission-rule reweighting aligns with this value if the technical assumptions hold; conditional on three unresolved constraints.
reactions.interest: Defence of talent-inclusive institutional design that serves the documented value of universal opportunity to develop talent, not just elite reproduction.
reactions.public_good_frame: A nation develops its full intellectual resource only if early selection targets ability, not family background. Selection rules that leak SES-advantage waste talent and fragment cohesion.
reactions.argument: S1 directly attacks the mechanism by which SES advantage converts to selective entry: it reweights selection rules away from preparation-sensitive instruments and introduces composition-balancing quotas. This aligns with the foundational claim that selection must be talent-driven. However, the scenario flags three load-bearing assumptions that our documented position depends on: (1) a workable, non-gameable SES indicator must exist at application time—Hungary currently has no administrative student-level SES register, and address-based or benefit-receipt proxies are imperfect and gameable; (2) non-state maintainers must be brought under uniform rules, which assumes subsidy-conditional leverage exists and will be exercised; (3) a less-coachable assessment must be buildable and validated in the reform's timeframe, and the scenario notes this requires multi-year psychometric development, not a low one-off cost. Our value commits us to supporting the direction if these constraints are resolved. We cannot claim the reform will work as modelled if they are not.
reactions.condition_to_change: We would shift to 'oppose' if: (a) the SES indicator becomes provably gameable at scale, making quota-targeting meaningless; or (b) non-state maintainers are exempted or subsidy-conditional leverage proves unenforceable, creating a two-tier system where selective demand migrates to unregulated maintainers. We would shift to 'support' (removing conditionality) if: piloting demonstrates a non-coachable assessment can be built within 2-3 years AND the SES-proxy withstands cross-validation against actual family-background data in the registry.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Egyensúly Intézet's 2021 model assumes universal primary cohesion up to a specified age. Mechanically reducing early-sorted share aligns with this principle. However, the scenario's expected benefits rest on a contested peer-effect and retention mechanism that the scenario itself labels weak/unevidenced, creating a mismatch between our value commitment and the evidence base offered.
reactions.interest: Defence of structural designs that keep high-ability pupils in common classrooms with lower-achieving peers, on the grounds that peer composition shapes opportunity for the disadvantaged majority.
reactions.public_good_frame: Concentrating high-achievers early in separate schools drains peer quality from the common track, systematically disadvantaging the majority left behind. Later sorting preserves cohesion longer.
reactions.argument: S2's mechanical effect—capping early-entry places—directly serves our value of extended common cohesion. The arithmetic of caps is direct and measurable. However, the scenario's expected downstream benefits (peer-composition improvement in urban general schools, family retention in the common track) rest on two contested links: (1) the asymmetric peer-effect mechanism is tagged contested/weak in the equity_and_social_mobility source; (2) the retention effect—that fewer selective seats will keep ambitious families in the common track if advanced-programme funding is available—is labeled unevidenced, and no source demonstrates that families currently exit for selective seats would actually stay if funded advanced programmes existed. The scenario itself notes that caps on state schools are mechanically offset if non-state maintainers expand selective intake. We can commit to the mechanical cap effect as a necessary condition, but not to the benefits modelled unless the retention and offset-prevention mechanisms are demonstrated.
reactions.condition_to_change: We would shift to 'support' (removing conditionality) if: piloted cap trajectories with parallel advanced-programme funding in at least two urban general schools show measurable retention of high-ability families in the common track AND registry data on non-state selective intake show no statistically significant surge offsetting the cap over a 3-5 year window. We would shift to 'oppose' if: (a) a successor government reverses the trajectory unpunished, demonstrating that legislative entrenchment cannot hold; or (b) non-state maintainers demonstrably absorb capped demand, making the public-sector cap a shell.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Egyensúly Intézet's 2021 model embeds the principle of universal primary cohesion as foundational to 'okos nemzet'. S3 directly operationalizes this: postponing selection to age 14 is the structural form of the principle. Cross-country evidence (Finnish, Polish delayed-selection reforms) supports equity gains. However, the scenario's mechanism depends on a contested peer-effect channel, and it carries a specific equity risk—middle-class exit to non-state schools—that directly undercuts the assumption that sorting does not migrate.
reactions.interest: Defence of comprehensive schooling to later ages as the structural prerequisite for talent-inclusive development and inter-generational mobility.
reactions.public_good_frame: Early selection at ages 10-12 locks parental background into academic trajectory before individual capacity can be reliably measured and before disadvantaged pupils have equal access to diagnostic information. A nation maximizes talent if selection is delayed and bundled with diagnostic support, not if it is outsourced to parental investment and test-prep.
reactions.argument: S3 directly embodies our foundational value: it extends common cohesion to age 14 and postpones between-school sorting until pupils have matured and information asymmetries have been partially closed. Cross-country evidence (Pekkarinen et al. 2009 on Finland; Jakubowski et al. 2016 on Poland) shows delayed selection reduces the SES-outcome gradient. However, the scenario flags two critical constraints that determine whether the reform is feasible or merely politically visible: (1) the load-bearing mechanism is a contested peer-effect: the scenario itself notes the asymmetric-peer-effect channel is tagged weak/contested in the equity source, and the claimed decade-scale weaker SES gradient depends on this channel being real and large enough; (2) sorting migration risk is conceded as 'the largest risk' in the equity_impact—middle-class families can exit to school choice within general schools or to non-state/private maintainers, concentrating disadvantage among the pupils left behind and undercutting the equity premise. The scenario's own assumption field concedes this migration is 'asserted here yet the equity_impact field calls exactly this migration the largest risk; it is load-bearing and unevidenced.' We value the structural direction but cannot model confidence in the equity outcome if the mechanism is contested and the largest downside risk is migration of sorting rather than removal of it.
reactions.condition_to_change: We would shift to 'support' (removing conditionality) if: (a) a Hungary-specific quasi-experimental or RCT design demonstrates that delayed selection reduces the SES-outcome gradient by at least 15-20 percentage points within 5-7 years, AND (b) parallel registry data show that non-state maintainer selective intake does not surge during the pilot, indicating that sorting does not simply migrate. We would shift to 'oppose' if: (a) pilot data show measurable middle-class exit to private schools without offsetting improvement in disadvantaged-pupil outcomes; or (b) teacher supply proves insufficient to deliver heterogeneous classrooms at scale, degrading academic quality in the common track and accelerating exit.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Egyensúly Intézet's 2021 documented position assumes structural integrity of common primary cohesion as a prerequisite for talent-inclusive development. S4 explicitly retains the sorting structure unchanged and relies on compensation—a fundamentally different theory of change that contradicts the documented value that selection at ages 10-12 is the problem, not its effects.
reactions.interest: Defence of structural reform that eliminates early selection, not compensation that accepts selection and layers palliatives on top.
reactions.public_good_frame: A nation does not become smart by compensating for sorting; it becomes smart by ensuring sorting does not happen at ages 10-12 in the first place. Compensation that leaves the sorting mechanism intact risks legitimising permanent stratification while masking its effects.
reactions.argument: S4 is fundamentally misaligned with our documented value. The 2021 'okos nemzet' model does not propose to accept early sorting and compensate for it downstream; it proposes to eliminate early sorting and ensure common cohesion. S4 keeps the structure unchanged and attempts to 'neutralize' its equity effects through TEIP-style targeted support and rigorous standards. This rests on two unresolved and likely unevidenced claims: (1) the scenario itself labels as 'weak' the assumption that compensation can 'offset' (not merely mask) the effects of early sorting at Hungary's selection age, explicitly noting that 'no country has demonstrated offsetting at Hungary's selection age' and that the Portuguese case did not run early selection at ages 10-12, so transfer is uncertain; (2) the scenario concedes in the political_risks field that compensation 'may entrench rather than dismantle the sorting engine'—i.e. that acceptance of the structure may legitimise permanent stratification. Beyond these mechanisms, S4 carries a budget vulnerability that undermines implementation: the scenario notes that 'compensation lines are the first cut in fiscal consolidation, so gains are reversible across fiscal cycles.' This means the package is not a stable institutional change but a cyclically vulnerable spending line. Our value commits us to structural reform that removes the sorting mechanism, not compensation that accepts it and gambles on protective funding cycles.
reactions.condition_to_change: We would shift to 'conditional' (not to 'support') only if: (a) a piloted TEIP-style package demonstrably narrows the SES-outcome gradient in disadvantaged-school cohorts by at least 20 percentage points within 4-5 years, AND (b) rigorous evidence shows that compensation has actually offset sorting effects (not merely masked them) AND (c) legislative ring-fencing mechanisms demonstrably protect the budget line across fiscal cycles in a multi-government context (e.g. comparative analysis of entrenchment durability). We would not shift to 'support' because even full success on these criteria would leave the structural sorting mechanism in place, contradicting the documented value that early selection is the problem.
